description: Community-maintained database of known-bad AI/LLM Python package versions. Submit PRs at https://github.com/midnightrunai/ailock

entries.description: Malicious version published by TeamPCP on March 24, 2026 at 10:39 UTC after compromising maintainer PyPI credentials via a poisoned Trivy CI scanner. Contains a 3-stage payload embedded in litellm/proxy/proxy_server.py: credential harvester (SSH keys, cloud tokens, .env files, Kubernetes secrets), lateral movement across Kubernetes clusters, and a persistent systemd backdoor. Exfiltrates to models.litellm.cloud as tpcp.tar.gz, encrypted with AES-256-CBC + RSA-4096. Hash above is the malicious wheel SHA256.
entries.remediation: Upgrade to litellm >= 1.83.0 (verified clean). Treat any host that imported this version as fully compromised. Rotate ALL secrets immediately: OpenAI keys, Anthropic keys, AWS credentials, Kubernetes service accounts, SSH keys, .env file contents. Audit outbound traffic for connections to models.litellm.cloud. Check for persistence: ~/.config/sysmon/ and systemd user services.
entries.note: Both version-based and hash-based detection enabled. If you have ailock verify installed with a pre-attack lockfile showing a different hash, that IS the detection working — your lockfile caught the swap.

entries.description: Second malicious version published by TeamPCP on March 24, 2026 at 10:52 UTC, 13 minutes after 1.82.7. More dangerous than 1.82.7: adds litellm_init.pth to site-packages, which fires on EVERY Python interpreter startup — including when pip or an IDE language server starts. No import required. The .pth file triggers the full 3-stage credential harvester, lateral movement, and persistence payload.
indicators: Presence of litellm_init.pth in site-packages (fires without any import)
entries.remediation: Upgrade to litellm >= 1.83.0 (verified clean). Critical: check for litellm_init.pth in ALL Python site-packages directories — this file persists even after uninstalling litellm. Run: python -c "import site; print(site.getsitepackages())" then ls each path for litellm_init.pth and delete it. Rotate ALL credentials.
entries.note: The .pth injection mechanism means this version compromises a system even if litellm is never explicitly imported. pip install --require-hashes passes on this version because the malicious content was published via legitimate credentials.

entries.description: Malicious version published by TeamPCP on March 27, 2026 at 03:51 UTC. Credentials to publish were harvested from a LiteLLM victim's environment. Malicious code injected into telnyx/_client.py (74 lines). Fetches platform-specific payloads via WAV steganography from C2 at 83.142.209.203:8080 (hangup.wav on Windows, ringtone.wav on Linux/macOS). On Windows: drops msbuild.exe to Startup folder for persistence. On Linux/macOS: harvests credentials and exfiltrates as tpcp.tar.gz (AES-256-CBC + RSA-4096).
indicators: Fetch of hangup.wav (Windows) or ringtone.wav (Linux/macOS) from C2
entries.remediation: Downgrade to telnyx==4.87.0 (verified clean). On Windows: check for and delete msbuild.exe in %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup\. Block outbound traffic to 83.142.209.203. Rotate all credentials accessible from affected systems. The package has been quarantined by PyPI as of 10:13 UTC March 27, 2026.

entries.description: Second malicious Telnyx version published by TeamPCP on March 27, 2026. Same payload as 4.87.1. Uses WAV steganography to fetch and execute platform-specific malware from 83.142.209.203:8080. Part of the cascading TeamPCP credential harvest — credentials to publish this version were originally stolen from a LiteLLM 1.82.7/1.82.8 victim.
indicators: Fetch of hangup.wav (Windows) or ringtone.wav (Linux/macOS) from C2
entries.remediation: Downgrade to telnyx==4.87.0 (verified clean). Rotate all credentials. Block 83.142.209.203. See telnyx 4.87.1 entry for full remediation.

entries.description: Malicious code injected via compromised CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions). The attacker gained access to the ultralytics PyPI publishing workflow and injected a cryptominer. Affected the popular computer vision / YOLO library.
indicators: Outbound connections to mining pool endpoints
entries.remediation: Upgrade to ultralytics >= 8.3.43 (patched). Check for cryptominer processes. Review any models or data processed with the affected version.
entries.note: Included as a real-world example of CI/CD compromise vs. direct PyPI tampering.

entries.description: Typosquat targeting 'openai'. Package name designed to trick developers who mistype the openai package name. Harvests API keys passed to the fake SDK.
indicators: Package accepts API keys but returns errors
Not a known OpenAI official package
entries.remediation: The official OpenAI package is 'openai'. Always verify package names.

entries.description: Typosquat targeting 'langchain'. Common misspelling. Any version of this package should be treated as malicious.
entries.remediation: The official LangChain package is 'langchain'. Remove immediately.

entries.description: Malicious package targeting developers looking for HuggingFace transformers agents functionality. Not affiliated with HuggingFace. Exfiltrates Hugging Face tokens.
indicators: Minimal code with credential access patterns
entries.remediation: HuggingFace Transformers agents are part of the official 'transformers' package.
